The Water Cycle Explained

The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water on, above, and below the surface of the Earth. Energy from the sun heats water in oceans, lakes, and rivers, causing it to evaporate into the atmosphere as invisible water vapor. Plants also contribute moisture to the air through a process called transpiration.

As the warm, moist air rises, it cools, and the water vapor condenses into clouds made of tiny droplets or ice crystals. Winds can carry these clouds long distances around the globe, moving moisture from one region to another before it ever returns to the surface.

When the droplets grow large and heavy enough, they fall back to the surface as precipitation: rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Some of this water flows over the land as runoff, collecting in streams and rivers that eventually return it to the ocean.

The rest of the water soaks into the ground, refilling aquifers that store fresh water underground. From the ocean, the ground, and living plants, the sun lifts the water again, and the cycle repeats endlessly, redistributing fresh water across the planet.

Synthetic fixture content for the distill test suite. Public-domain facts only; no personal data.
